channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: The View From Europe
item.description: Belgian Defense Minister Theo Francken on Hegseth, Hormuz, and NATO.

item.title: Iran War Nears 60-Day War Powers Deadline
item.description: But U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth argues that the cease-fire has paused the clock.

item.title: Giorgia Meloni’s Populist Formula Failed
item.description: The Italian prime minister hasn’t convincingly delivered the renewal she once promised.

item.title: High-Rises Amid the Rubble in Syria
item.description: Who is Sharaa’s investment campaign really for?

item.title: ‘Everything Depends on Our Attitudes’
item.description: Belgian Defense Minister Theo Francken on Hegseth, Hormuz, and NATO’s future.

item.title: Time to edit some biological metaphors
item.description: Not everything is in your DNA

item.title: The Real Private-Credit Risk Is Opacity, Not Leverage
item.description: Credit risk is migrating beyond the traditional banking system into a sprawling network of funds, insurers, and lenders, with no single authority able to see the whole picture. The challenge facing policymakers is to ensure that risks remain visible as they shift into corners of the financial system not designed to absorb them.

item.title: Former Kyrgyz Security Chief Charged With Coup-plotting and Abuse of Office
item.description: Tashiev has not yet been detained, but has reportedly been instructed not to leave town.

item.title: How the Dreams of Bangladesh’s Student Protestors Died Young
item.description: Almost two years after Bangladesh's July uprising, its foot soldiers are learning what it means when a revolution eats its own.

item.title: US Deep-Sea Mining Policy Is Eroding Its Pacific Partnerships
item.description: The current unilateral approach may secure minerals in the short term, but it will erode the Pacific partnerships and deterrence architecture the United States needs for long-term security.

item.title: A New Economics for the 21st Century
item.description: By coming out in favor of industrial policy after many decades of advising against government intervention in the economy, the World Bank has taken an important step. But it still has much further to go to translate real-world evidence into better policy advice.

item.title: Warning Signs: How China Normalizes Its Military Presence
item.description: As its presence becomes more normal, China’s aggressive and illegal behavior will attract less opposition.

item.title: Pakistan Moves Toward a Sea-Denial Strategy
item.description: With its latest missile tests, the Pakistan Navy is operationalizing an A2/AD naval strategy that prioritizes precision strike capabilities.

item.title: The AI supply crunch is here
item.description: Choke points on the supply of AI are changing its economics

item.title: Is Samia Suluhu Hassan Africa’s most disappointing president?
item.description: A sham election, a massacre whitewashed

item.title: Climate change is forcing Vanuatu to confront an unthinkable future
item.description: Legal battles abroad offer little comfort

item.title: Is Vietnam’s latest railway ambition worthwhile?
item.description: Some say the whopping project should be scaled down

item.title: Cai Qi may be China’s second-most powerful man
item.description: Xi Jinping’s chief of staff handles his security, schedule and more

item.title: Hong Kong is a good place to find stolen or looted Chinese artefacts
item.description: The financial hub abounds with precious wares

item.title: China is seeking self-sufficiency in police dogs
item.description: State media are singing the praises of home-grown canines

item.title: Countries are rushing to build ports in a contest to secure maritime trade routes
item.description: The investment frenzy is driven by anxiety about China’s tightening grip on supply chains

item.title: The Southern Poverty Law Centre has badly lost its way
item.description: But the government’s lawsuit against it is still bonkers

item.title: Voters say they want young candidates. In practice, they do not
item.description: The Massachusetts Senate primary offers a case study

item.title: Business
The weekly cartoon
Politics
The oligarch who picked Moldova clean goes to prison
item.description: Vlad Plahotniuc ran his country as a private money-laundering operation

item.title: AI and the danger of cognitive surrender
item.description: How much should managers let bots do the thinking?

item.title: How a mega-deal will transform the lift industry
item.description: Together Kone and TKE will tower over their rivals

item.title: African finance goes global
item.description: The continent’s fintechs want to ease cross-border payments

item.title: Hizbullah’s air of invincibility is gone
item.description: But it still has the power to threaten Lebanon’s leaders

item.title: The lack of progress in Gaza suits those in power
item.description: But it leaves Gazans in misery

item.title: A tour of Brazil’s wildly polarised politics
item.description: Lula and Bolsonaro voters don’t agree on much. But they all hate the corruption that plagues Brazil

item.title: The Caribbean island that calls Colombia a coloniser
item.description: Locals on San Andrés blame the mainland for crime and corruption

item.title: The government’s lawsuit against the Southern Poverty Law Center is bonkers
item.description: But the non-profit had badly lost its way

item.title: Hong Kong is a conduit for looted Chinese antiquities
item.description: The financial hub abounds with precious wares

item.title: The global scrmble for ports
item.description: The investment frenzy is driven by anxiety about China’s tightening grip on supply chains

item.title: The global scramble for ports
item.description: The investment frenzy is driven by anxiety about China’s tightening grip on supply chains

item.title: China’s Economy: By the Numbers
item.description: Officially, China's economy is on the rise yet again. But there are other sources of data to consider.

item.title: Is the EU the Next Migration Destination for Uzbeks?
item.description: EU countries are becoming increasingly attractive destinations for Uzbek migrants, driven by higher wages, stronger labor protections, and adherence to international labor standards.

item.title: An Ayatollah for the Aggrieved
item.description: For Iran’s Sunni admirers, resistance remains the appeal.

item.title: An Economic Model for Securing Hormuz
item.description: With a military solution to the closure of the Strait unlikely to succeed, the only sustainable long-term arrangement is to institutionalize today’s emerging arrangement, by which Iran, in coordination with the Gulf states, guarantees safe passage for a fee. Such a system could benefit all parties—not least the United States.

item.title: Australia’s Foreign Minister Makes Critical Visits to Northeast Asia
item.description: Stopping in Japan, South Korea, and China, Foreign Minister Penny Wong met with three of Australia’s four largest trading partners.

item.title: Who Lost America’s Universities?
item.description: Lost in all the debates about whether US higher education is serving its proper function are conservative critics’ own motives. Leveraging the public’s apparent loss of confidence in colleges and universities, the American right has shifted the discussion from academic freedom to free speech, using the latter to undermine the former.

item.title: The crisis in oil markets will get bigger before it goes away
item.description: As stocks dwindle, further price rises are inevitable

item.title: The UAE walks out of OPEC
item.description: And doubles down on Israel and America

item.title: The UAE doubles down on Israel and America
item.description: The consequences of the Emirates’ departure from OPEC

item.title: Can countries grow richer by exporting people, not goods?
item.description: That depends where those people end up

item.title: India’s weak currency reflects deeper problems than the Iran war
item.description: It highlight a persistent inability to draw in foreign investors

item.title: How Kalshi can help the Federal Reserve
item.description: Prediction markets may be a win for central banking

item.title: How to capitalise on London’s thriving financial industry
item.description: The City has bounced back despite fears over Brexit

item.title: Elon Musk and Sam Altman bring their rivalry to court
item.description: Of the two unloved billionaires, which will the jury trust?

item.title: The Eulogy That Explains Israel’s Current Tragedy
item.description: Israel has forgotten the wisdom in an iconic funeral oration delivered 70 years ago.

item.title: Oil markets are still in La La land
item.description: Prices have risen sharply. Unfortunately, they still have further to go

item.title: What Consultation Looks Like on a Cambodian Carbon Offset Project
item.description: A REDD+ project in the country's northeast aims to conserve forest and reduce carbon emissions, but many local residents say they know little about it and never gave their consent.

item.title: Former Thai PM Thaksin Approved for Parole, Set For Release on May 11
item.description: The decision could pave the way for the 76-year-old's return to politics, but the future of his Pheu Thai Party is uncertain following a poor return at this year's general election.

item.title: For What AI Could Do to Democracies, Look to the Petrostates
item.description: Societies will become richer, but history suggests that wealth may not be equally distributed.

item.title: The New Resource Curse
item.description: Critical minerals will scramble geopolitics.

item.title: What Drove Down America’s Fentanyl Deaths?
item.description: China might have—but wouldn’t want Washington to know.

item.title: In the Fight Against Southeast Asia-based Scam Operations, Greater Protections and Reforms Are Needed
item.description: Washington has finally woken up to the scale of the challenge, but there is a lot more it can do to punish perpetrators and support victims.

item.title: Where Do Thailand-China Relations Stand in 2026?
item.description: This year is set to witness a flowering of relations between Bangkok and Beijing – but Thai strategists remain both ambivalent and wary.

item.title: US Court Dismisses Rohingya Hate Speech Lawsuit Against Meta
item.description: Plaintiffs argued that Facebook helped spread hate speech that “amounted to a substantial cause, and eventual perpetuation of, the Rohingya genocide.”

item.title: A new coalition should oust Binyamin Netanyahu
item.description: But Israel’s prime minister still has one big advantage
